title: Placental Abruption
oneLiner: Bleeding at placental-decidual interface causing abruption; obstetric emergency with maternal-fetal risk; onset often sudden, severity variable.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)** — if abruption unrecognised, concealed bleeding + DIC → sudden collapse. Single finding: **continuous vaginal bleeding with hard, non-relaxing uterus** (Couvelaire uterus) + maternal tachycardia out of proportion to visible blood loss.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** — abruption releases thromboplastin into maternal circulation → consumptive coagulopathy. Single finding: **bleeding from IV sites, gums, or petechiae** with prolonged PT/aPTT and low fibrinogen.
- **Fetal distress / intrauterine death** — the fetus depends on placental circulation; abruption cuts oxygen delivery. Single finding: **abnormal CTG** (late decelerations, sinusoidal pattern, or persistently absent variability) in a pregnant patient presenting with abdominal pain and/or bleeding.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5):**
  - Vaginal bleeding (red/dark, can be concealed) — **but up to 20% have no visible bleeding** (concealed abruption)
  - Constant, severe abdominal pain (often “board-like” uterus)
  - Uterine hypertonus / frequent contractions (rigid uterus on palpation)
  - Back pain (especially with posterior placenta)
  - Reduced fetal movements (patient reports “baby less active”)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Uterine tenderness and rigidity (may be localised or generalised)
  - Vaginal bleeding (assess amount; use pads/sanitary towels for quantification)
  - Fetal bradycardia or absent heart sounds (doppler/CTG)
  - Signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) — often late in concealed abruption because blood loss is underestimated.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Placenta praevia** — painless bright red bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, high presenting part. **Splitter:** **Ultrasound to localise placenta** — if placenta covers internal os = praevia; if not = abruption.
- **Preterm labour / threatened miscarriage** — contractions, bleeding, but pain is intermittent (not constant) and uterus is not rigid.
- **Vasa praevia** — painless, minimal bleeding with fetal heart rate abnormalities; rare. Diagnosis by doppler on USS.
- **Uterine rupture** — previous caesarean section, sudden severe pain, fetal bradycardia, maternal collapse. Exclude by history and USS.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - CTG (continuous fetal monitoring) — trace for variability, decelerations, baseline. If <26 weeks, document fetal heart rate but not formal CTG.
  - NEWS2 (maternal) and fetal heart rate.
  - Vaginal examination **only if placenta praevia excluded** (USS or clinical suspicion). If praevia not excluded: **no speculum or digital exam** (risk of catastrophic haemorrhage).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer), crossmatch (2 units), U&E, LFT, group & screen (Rh status if <36 weeks).
  - **Fibrinogen <2 g/L** strongly suggests DIC; falling levels indicate progression.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Ultrasound** (transabdominal) to locate placenta (rule out praevia) and assess for retroplacental clot. **USS has low sensitivity for abruption** (~50%), so negative USS does not exclude.
  - Second-line: **MRI** if stable and diagnostic uncertainty (rarely acute indication).
- **Special tests:**
  - Kleihauer test (if Rh-negative) to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and guide anti-D dosing.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical grading of abruption** (Page’s classification):
    - Grade 1: mild bleeding, no fetal distress, no coagulopathy.
    - Grade 2: moderate bleeding, uterine hypertonus, fetal distress (CTG abnormalities).
    - Grade 3: severe bleeding (may be concealed), maternal shock, fetal death, coagulopathy present.
- **Never-miss test:** **Ultrasound to rule out placenta praevia before any vaginal examination** (or careful documentation if impossible). Missing that step → massive haemorrhage.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitation:** Call obstetric senior (ST6+/consultant) and anaesthetist. IV access × 2 wide-bore (14–16G). Start warmed crystalloid (Hartmann’s / normal saline 500 mL bolus, then titrate). Crossmatch and transfuse O-negative blood if haemodynamically unstable and crossmatching not yet complete.
  - **Monitoring:** Continuous CTG (if >26 weeks), maternal pulse/blood pressure every 5 min initially, urine output (catheterise).
  - **Assess severity:** Grade 1–2 (maternal stable, fetal viable) → plan for delivery (usually caesarean section if <37 weeks or fetal distress). Grade 3 (fetal death, maternal DIC) → aim for vaginal delivery as soon as possible to prevent worsening DIC; caesarean reserved for failed progress or maternal compromise.
  - **Administer corticosteroids if preterm (<34+6 weeks)** — betamethasone 12 mg IM stat, repeat 24 hours later (if delivery not imminent within 2 hours). Check [local policy] for exact timing.
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin** if Rh-negative mother — 500 IU IM (or higher dose if Kleihauer positive, up to [dose per local policy] for >4 mL fetomaternal haemorrhage).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **If expectant management (grade 1, mother stable, no fetal distress):** Inpatient care on Labour Ward. Serial abruption scoring, daily FBC/coagulation, CTG monitoring. Strict bed rest not proven beneficial, but limit activity.
  - **If delivered:** Monitor for PPH — give oxytocin infusion (40 IU in 500 mL normal saline at 10–40 mL/hour titrated to uterine tone). Assess blood loss. Repeat coagulation screen; if DIC persists, involve haematology.
  - **Pain:** Paracetamol 1 g IV 6-hourly; morphine IV (2.5–5 mg) for severe pain (watch for maternal respiratory depression).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If early abruption (<24 weeks) and pregnancy continues: follow-up with serial growth scans (every 2–4 weeks) and antenatal care. Risk of recurrence in future pregnancies (5–10%).
  - **If fetal death:** Psychological support, bereavement counselling.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV cannula (keep patent). Oral fluids if stable and no imminent delivery.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Mechanical (TED stockings, sequential compression devices) if peripartum. Enoxaparin after delivery if no bleeding risk — start 4–6 hours post-delivery ([check local policy] for dose).
  - **Diet/NBM:** If delivery likely (grade 2–3), keep NBM for emergency caesarean.
  - **Observation frequency:** Maternal NEWS2 hourly, CTG continuous if fetal viability; vaginal blood loss assessment (pad count) every 15–30 min initially.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity:** Betamethasone 12 mg IM single dose, repeat 24 hours later if delivery not within 2 hours. Dexamethasone alternative: 6 mg IM 12-hourly × 4 doses.
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** 500 IU IM (Rh-negative, non-sensitised). For fetomaternal haemorrhage >4 mL, dose per local policy (often 1500 IU or more based on Kleihauer).
  - **Oxytocin for labour induction/postpartum:** 5–10 IU slow IV bolus (if haemodynamically stable) or 40 IU in 500 mL NS at 10–40 mL/hr.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Carboprost (prostaglandin F2α)** for PPH refractory to oxytocin: 250 µg IM every 15–90 min (max 2 mg).
  - **Tranexamic acid** 1 g IV over 10 min (if ongoing PPH, consider within 3 hours of onset).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Anti-D: no contraindication in abruption — essential if Rh-negative.
  - Corticosteroids: caution in maternal infection (sepsis) — weigh benefits.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not applicable (short-term use).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxytocin — never give as undiluted IV bolus >10 IU** (risk of severe hypotension, arrhythmia). Always dilute and infuse.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - DIC (consumptive coagulopathy) — check fibrinogen, cryoprecipitate if <1 g/L (dose 5–10 units; [check local policy]).
  - Maternal haemorrhagic shock → acute kidney injury, Sheehan’s syndrome.
  - Fetal hypoxia → intrauterine death, neonatal acidosis.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence in subsequent pregnancies (5–10%).
  - Long-term maternal renal impairment if severe shock.
  - Psychological trauma (PTSD) after fetal loss.
sections.Prognosis: - Maternal mortality low (<1%) with modern care, but significant morbidity (DIC, transfusion, hysterectomy). Fetal mortality 10–15% overall; >50% if Grade 3 (fetal death). Early recognition and delivery improve outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a bedside ultrasound to locate placenta before any vaginal examination.** One quick scan can prevent catastrophic haemorrhage from undiagnosed praevia.
- **Start IV access and crossmatch immediately** in any pregnant patient with vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain — do not wait for investigations.
- **Check fibrinogen early** (within 30 minutes) if abruption suspected; falling levels predict DIC and need for cryoprecipitate.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not perform a vaginal or speculum examination until placenta praevia is ruled out.** This is the most dangerous error.
- **Do not underestimate concealed abruption** — a hard, tender uterus with normal vital signs can rapidly progress to DIC. Monitor serial coagulation.
- **Do not delay delivery** in grade 2–3 abruption hoping for fetal lung maturation. The fetus is better delivered than subjected to ischaemia.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Any increase in vaginal bleeding (soaking >1 pad per hour), new constant abdominal pain, decreased fetal movements, dizziness, palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** Obstetric triage within 24–48 hours after discharge for clinical review and USS. If ongoing bleeding, admit.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (obstetric registrar / consultant) immediately if:**
  - Grade 2 or greater abruption suspected.
  - Maternal tachycardia >110 bpm or hypotension (SBP <100 mmHg).
  - NEWS2 ≥5.
  - Fetal bradycardia (<110 bpm) or absent heart sounds.
  - Abruption with fetal death → consultant-led care.
  - Suspicion of DIC (bleeding from venepuncture sites, petechiae, prolonged coagulation, low fibrinogen).
- **Call crash team:** Maternal collapse / cardiac arrest (treat as per obstetric cardiac arrest drill — left uterine displacement, remove fetal monitoring, consider perimortem caesarean section if >20 weeks and no ROSC in 4 minutes).

rawMarkdown:
## Placental Abruption
**One-liner:** Bleeding at placental-decidual interface causing abruption; obstetric emergency with maternal-fetal risk; onset often sudden, severity variable.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)** — if abruption unrecognised, concealed bleeding + DIC → sudden collapse. Single finding: **continuous vaginal bleeding with hard, non-relaxing uterus** (Couvelaire uterus) + maternal tachycardia out of proportion to visible blood loss.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** — abruption releases thromboplastin into maternal circulation → consumptive coagulopathy. Single finding: **bleeding from IV sites, gums, or petechiae** with prolonged PT/aPTT and low fibrinogen.
- **Fetal distress / intrauterine death** — the fetus depends on placental circulation; abruption cuts oxygen delivery. Single finding: **abnormal CTG** (late decelerations, sinusoidal pattern, or persistently absent variability) in a pregnant patient presenting with abdominal pain and/or bleeding.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5):**
  - Vaginal bleeding (red/dark, can be concealed) — **but up to 20% have no visible bleeding** (concealed abruption)
  - Constant, severe abdominal pain (often “board-like” uterus)
  - Uterine hypertonus / frequent contractions (rigid uterus on palpation)
  - Back pain (especially with posterior placenta)
  - Reduced fetal movements (patient reports “baby less active”)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Uterine tenderness and rigidity (may be localised or generalised)
  - Vaginal bleeding (assess amount; use pads/sanitary towels for quantification)
  - Fetal bradycardia or absent heart sounds (doppler/CTG)
  - Signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) — often late in concealed abruption because blood loss is underestimated.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Placenta praevia** — painless bright red bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, high presenting part. **Splitter:** **Ultrasound to localise placenta** — if placenta covers internal os = praevia; if not = abruption.
- **Preterm labour / threatened miscarriage** — contractions, bleeding, but pain is intermittent (not constant) and uterus is not rigid.
- **Vasa praevia** — painless, minimal bleeding with fetal heart rate abnormalities; rare. Diagnosis by doppler on USS.
- **Uterine rupture** — previous caesarean section, sudden severe pain, fetal bradycardia, maternal collapse. Exclude by history and USS.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - CTG (continuous fetal monitoring) — trace for variability, decelerations, baseline. If <26 weeks, document fetal heart rate but not formal CTG.
  - NEWS2 (maternal) and fetal heart rate.
  - Vaginal examination **only if placenta praevia excluded** (USS or clinical suspicion). If praevia not excluded: **no speculum or digital exam** (risk of catastrophic haemorrhage).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer), crossmatch (2 units), U&E, LFT, group & screen (Rh status if <36 weeks).
  - **Fibrinogen <2 g/L** strongly suggests DIC; falling levels indicate progression.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Ultrasound** (transabdominal) to locate placenta (rule out praevia) and assess for retroplacental clot. **USS has low sensitivity for abruption** (~50%), so negative USS does not exclude.
  - Second-line: **MRI** if stable and diagnostic uncertainty (rarely acute indication).
- **Special tests:**
  - Kleihauer test (if Rh-negative) to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and guide anti-D dosing.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical grading of abruption** (Page’s classification):
    - Grade 1: mild bleeding, no fetal distress, no coagulopathy.
    - Grade 2: moderate bleeding, uterine hypertonus, fetal distress (CTG abnormalities).
    - Grade 3: severe bleeding (may be concealed), maternal shock, fetal death, coagulopathy present.
- **Never-miss test:** **Ultrasound to rule out placenta praevia before any vaginal examination** (or careful documentation if impossible). Missing that step → massive haemorrhage.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitation:** Call obstetric senior (ST6+/consultant) and anaesthetist. IV access × 2 wide-bore (14–16G). Start warmed crystalloid (Hartmann’s / normal saline 500 mL bolus, then titrate). Crossmatch and transfuse O-negative blood if haemodynamically unstable and crossmatching not yet complete.
  - **Monitoring:** Continuous CTG (if >26 weeks), maternal pulse/blood pressure every 5 min initially, urine output (catheterise).
  - **Assess severity:** Grade 1–2 (maternal stable, fetal viable) → plan for delivery (usually caesarean section if <37 weeks or fetal distress). Grade 3 (fetal death, maternal DIC) → aim for vaginal delivery as soon as possible to prevent worsening DIC; caesarean reserved for failed progress or maternal compromise.
  - **Administer corticosteroids if preterm (<34+6 weeks)** — betamethasone 12 mg IM stat, repeat 24 hours later (if delivery not imminent within 2 hours). Check [local policy] for exact timing.
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin** if Rh-negative mother — 500 IU IM (or higher dose if Kleihauer positive, up to [dose per local policy] for >4 mL fetomaternal haemorrhage).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **If expectant management (grade 1, mother stable, no fetal distress):** Inpatient care on Labour Ward. Serial abruption scoring, daily FBC/coagulation, CTG monitoring. Strict bed rest not proven beneficial, but limit activity.
  - **If delivered:** Monitor for PPH — give oxytocin infusion (40 IU in 500 mL normal saline at 10–40 mL/hour titrated to uterine tone). Assess blood loss. Repeat coagulation screen; if DIC persists, involve haematology.
  - **Pain:** Paracetamol 1 g IV 6-hourly; morphine IV (2.5–5 mg) for severe pain (watch for maternal respiratory depression).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If early abruption (<24 weeks) and pregnancy continues: follow-up with serial growth scans (every 2–4 weeks) and antenatal care. Risk of recurrence in future pregnancies (5–10%).
  - **If fetal death:** Psychological support, bereavement counselling.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV cannula (keep patent). Oral fluids if stable and no imminent delivery.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Mechanical (TED stockings, sequential compression devices) if peripartum. Enoxaparin after delivery if no bleeding risk — start 4–6 hours post-delivery ([check local policy] for dose).
  - **Diet/NBM:** If delivery likely (grade 2–3), keep NBM for emergency caesarean.
  - **Observation frequency:** Maternal NEWS2 hourly, CTG continuous if fetal viability; vaginal blood loss assessment (pad count) every 15–30 min initially.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity:** Betamethasone 12 mg IM single dose, repeat 24 hours later if delivery not within 2 hours. Dexamethasone alternative: 6 mg IM 12-hourly × 4 doses.
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** 500 IU IM (Rh-negative, non-sensitised). For fetomaternal haemorrhage >4 mL, dose per local policy (often 1500 IU or more based on Kleihauer).
  - **Oxytocin for labour induction/postpartum:** 5–10 IU slow IV bolus (if haemodynamically stable) or 40 IU in 500 mL NS at 10–40 mL/hr.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Carboprost (prostaglandin F2α)** for PPH refractory to oxytocin: 250 µg IM every 15–90 min (max 2 mg).
  - **Tranexamic acid** 1 g IV over 10 min (if ongoing PPH, consider within 3 hours of onset).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Anti-D: no contraindication in abruption — essential if Rh-negative.
  - Corticosteroids: caution in maternal infection (sepsis) — weigh benefits.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not applicable (short-term use).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxytocin — never give as undiluted IV bolus >10 IU** (risk of severe hypotension, arrhythmia). Always dilute and infuse.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - DIC (consumptive coagulopathy) — check fibrinogen, cryoprecipitate if <1 g/L (dose 5–10 units; [check local policy]).
  - Maternal haemorrhagic shock → acute kidney injury, Sheehan’s syndrome.
  - Fetal hypoxia → intrauterine death, neonatal acidosis.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence in subsequent pregnancies (5–10%).
  - Long-term maternal renal impairment if severe shock.
  - Psychological trauma (PTSD) after fetal loss.

### Prognosis
- Maternal mortality low (<1%) with modern care, but significant morbidity (DIC, transfusion, hysterectomy). Fetal mortality 10–15% overall; >50% if Grade 3 (fetal death). Early recognition and delivery improve outcomes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a bedside ultrasound to locate placenta before any vaginal examination.** One quick scan can prevent catastrophic haemorrhage from undiagnosed praevia.
- **Start IV access and crossmatch immediately** in any pregnant patient with vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain — do not wait for investigations.
- **Check fibrinogen early** (within 30 minutes) if abruption suspected; falling levels predict DIC and need for cryoprecipitate.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not perform a vaginal or speculum examination until placenta praevia is ruled out.** This is the most dangerous error.
- **Do not underestimate concealed abruption** — a hard, tender uterus with normal vital signs can rapidly progress to DIC. Monitor serial coagulation.
- **Do not delay delivery** in grade 2–3 abruption hoping for fetal lung maturation. The fetus is better delivered than subjected to ischaemia.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Any increase in vaginal bleeding (soaking >1 pad per hour), new constant abdominal pain, decreased fetal movements, dizziness, palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** Obstetric triage within 24–48 hours after discharge for clinical review and USS. If ongoing bleeding, admit.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (obstetric registrar / consultant) immediately if:**
  - Grade 2 or greater abruption suspected.
  - Maternal tachycardia >110 bpm or hypotension (SBP <100 mmHg).
  - NEWS2 ≥5.
  - Fetal bradycardia (<110 bpm) or absent heart sounds.
  - Abruption with fetal death → consultant-led care.
  - Suspicion of DIC (bleeding from venepuncture sites, petechiae, prolonged coagulation, low fibrinogen).
- **Call crash team:** Maternal collapse / cardiac arrest (treat as per obstetric cardiac arrest drill — left uterine displacement, remove fetal monitoring, consider perimortem caesarean section if >20 weeks and no ROSC in 4 minutes).
